post.title: Decoding POTUS' New 10% Tariff: A Bold Pivot Under Section 122 of the Trade Act!!
post.excerpt: In the immediate aftermath of the U.S. Supreme Court’s Feb 20, 2026 decision curbing IEEPA- based “global/reciprocal” tariffs, Donald Trump has reached for a different lever: a temporary, across-the-board 10% import surcharge for 150 days under Section 122 of...
post.body:
In the immediate aftermath of the U.S. Supreme Court’s Feb 20, 2026 decision curbing IEEPA- based “global/reciprocal” tariffs, Donald Trump has reached for a different lever: a temporary, across-the-board 10% import surcharge for 150 days under Section 122 of the Trade Act of 1974, over and above tariffs already in place. The signal is clear—trade policy isn’t retreating; it’s being re-engineered inside the guardrails the Court just drew. Overview of Section 122 Section 122 (19 U.S.C. § 2132) was designed for moments when the U.S. faces “large and serious” balance-of-payments deficits or other “fundamental international payments problems.” It allows the President to impose a temporary import surcharge (up to 15%) or quotas—but only for up to 150 days unless U.S. Congress extends it. Unlike Section 301/232 pathways, it doesn’t require a formal investigation, and it’s never been used, meaning courts have had little reason to interpret its limits—until now. Interpretation of the 10% Tariff Under Section 122 The administration’s theory is straightforward: persistent external imbalances justify temporary action—without the long lead times of Section 301 investigations or Section 232 processes. The 10% level (below the 15% ceiling) functions as a stop-gap replacement while the White House simultaneously tees up longer-horizon tools—especially new Section 301 probes—aimed at a more targeted, more durable tariff architecture. Implications for Trade In the short run, a flat surcharge behaves like sand in the gears: higher landed costs, tighter margins, and faster re-pricing across manufacturing inputs, retail inventories, and agri/food

supply chains. Evidence from recent tariff pass-through suggests U.S. firms and consumers bear most of the cost, even when policy messaging says otherwise. The second-order risk is retaliation: partners may respond selectively—amplifying supply-chain churn and re-routing trade flows rather than “bringing jobs back” cleanly. Potential Legal Challenges Expect challenges on two fronts: Statutory fit — do today’s deficits meet Section 122’s “large and serious” threshold in a floating- exchange-rate world? Major-questions / delegation logic — after the Court’s strict reading of IEEPA, litigants may argue Section 122 cannot be stretched into a de facto permanent tariff regime. Internationally, World Trade Organization balance-of-payments rules could also be tested if the measure is framed as a BOP remedy. This is a pivotal moment in the evolving contest between executive speed and constitutional design. Is Section 122 a smart bridge—or the start of a new legal and economic whiplash cycle?
post.author: Know Your Commodity
